title: ML System Design
description: Recommendation, fraud, ranking, batch and real-time inference, feature stores, registries, and monitoring.

questions.title: Design a recommendation system.
questions.scenario: An e-commerce company wants personalized product recommendations.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong design covers candidate generation, ranking, user/item features, feedback loops, online and offline metrics, cold start, exploration, serving latency, feature freshness, experimentation, and monitoring. Candidates should discuss business objectives and guardrails. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: An e-commerce company wants personalized product recommendations. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by turning the concept into architecture: define inputs, data flow, ownership boundaries, controls, evaluation signals, observability, and rollout steps for ml system design.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "Design a recommendation system.", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real ml system design setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Skipping the scenario and giving a generic textbook explanation.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for ML System Design?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise ml system design setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: Design a fraud detection system.
questions.scenario: A payments company needs to score transactions in under 100 ms.
questions.expectedAnswer: Include real-time feature generation, model scoring, rules, risk thresholds, human review, feedback labels, drift monitoring, adversarial behavior, latency constraints, and explainability. Strong answers balance fraud capture with false-positive customer impact. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A payments company needs to score transactions in under 100 ms. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by turning the concept into architecture: define inputs, data flow, ownership boundaries, controls, evaluation signals, observability, and rollout steps for ml system design.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "Design a fraud detection system.", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real ml system design setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

questions.title: Compare batch inference and real-time inference.
questions.scenario: A churn model runs weekly, while fraud detection must run immediately.
questions.expectedAnswer: Batch inference is efficient for periodic scoring and high throughput. Real-time inference is needed when predictions must react immediately to user or event context. Tradeoffs include latency, freshness, infrastructure complexity, cost, and monitoring. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A churn model runs weekly, while fraud detection must run immediately. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by mapping the concept to a concrete workflow, choosing the simplest suitable technique, validating it with data or user feedback, and explaining how it changes the product or platform behavior.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "Compare batch inference and real-time inference.", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real ml system design setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

questions.title: What problem does a feature store solve?
questions.scenario: Multiple models compute customer risk features differently.
questions.expectedAnswer: A feature store manages reusable features, online/offline consistency, feature lineage, freshness, discovery, and access control. It helps avoid training-serving skew and duplicate feature logic. It is valuable when many teams share features across models. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.title: What should you monitor in production ML?
questions.scenario: A model’s accuracy quietly degrades after a market change.
questions.expectedAnswer: Monitor service health, latency, throughput, prediction distributions, feature drift, data quality, model performance, fairness, business metrics, feedback labels, and incidents. Monitoring should trigger investigation and retraining workflows when thresholds are breached. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.title: Design around search ranking system.
questions.scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through search ranking system for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define search ranking system, explain why it matters for ml system design, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. The candidate should describe the core mechanism, identify where it appears in a production system, discuss tradeoffs such as quality, cost, latency, safety, governance, and maintainability, and name how they would evaluate whether the approach is working. For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through model rollback strategy for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by turning the concept into architecture: define inputs, data flow, ownership boundaries, controls, evaluation signals, observability, and rollout steps for ml system design.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "Design around model rollback strategy.", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real ml system design setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Skipping the scenario and giving a generic textbook explanation.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for ML System Design?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise ml system design setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.
